summary: I was raised in Yugoslavia, a communist country where religion was not openly encouraged, though my parents still celebrated Christian holidays. During the war there, I saw religious identities become more pronounced as people sought safety within their groups. The conflict also made me question religion, especially when religious leaders led people into war. I believe things might have been better if Yugoslavic politicians had not discouraged religion so much.

After resettling in the United States, I was struck by the freedom to practice religion openly, which was a new and eye-opening experience for me. Here, people can choose their faith freely, something I consider a fundamental human right. America has become home to me, especially because of my children and grandchildren, and we celebrate both traditional American holidays like Thanksgiving and maintain ties to our cultural roots. The diversity and freedom here have allowed me to grow spiritually and culturally.

I worked closely with World Relief as a case manager, where I saw how faith-based support helped refugees cope with their losses from the war. Members of World Relief would pray together, and that spiritual connection made the impossible seem possible to me. This organization helped me engage with the Bible and find meaning in the stories, especially after witnessing human suffering during the conflict. World Relief's faith-based approach provided comfort that secular agencies sometimes lacked.

entity: United Nations International Children's Emergency Fund
summary: While working at UNICEF, I had a Muslim colleague, and despite our different faiths, we would ask each other to pray, showing me the power of interfaith respect. My role at UNICEF involved supporting refugees, many of whom had lost loved ones in the war, and I witnessed how shared struggles brought people together beyond religious differences. This experience deepened my understanding of faith as a unifying force in difficult times.

My son married an Indian woman, and through their relationship, I have come to appreciate Hinduism and the cultural events they observe. This has enriched our family life and broadened my perspective on faith and traditions. While I was raised Christian, celebrating Hindu events alongside American holidays like Thanksgiving has shown me the beauty of religious diversity within my own family.

Thanksgiving has become an important holiday for my family here in the United States, blending with our cultural and religious traditions. We celebrate it alongside other events, which helps us feel connected to American life while maintaining our heritage. This holiday symbolizes the freedom and new beginnings we have experienced since moving here.
